title: Alnylam — ATTR — briefing Q3-2025
html:
ATTR Amyloidosis Briefing: Q3 2025

Quarter Headline

AMVUTTRA's ATTR-CM launch hits inflection: Q2 2025 revenue reaches $492M (surpassing $350M consensus), HELIOS-B long-term data presented at ESC Congress 2025, and Alnylam raises 2025 TTR guidance for the second time.

Key Developments

1. AMVUTTRA Revenue Explosion

  • Q2 2025 AMVUTTRA revenue: $492M, smashing consensus by >40%
  • ~1,400 patients on therapy by end of Q2 2025 -- rapid uptake since March 2025 ATTR-CM approval
  • Alnylam raised 2025 TTR franchise guidance twice, reflecting stronger-than-expected ATTR-CM launch dynamics
  • 103% YoY TTR franchise growth in Q3 2025 (net product revenue)

What Alnylam management said on the Q3 2025 call:

  1. "We are in the early innings of the ATTR-CM opportunity" — management framed the entire ATTR-CM market as nascent despite already commanding ~$750M quarterly TTR revenue, signaling years of expected growth ahead.
  2. Highlighted >12,000 patients on AMVUTTRA with 99% US coverage, positioning the drug as the de facto standard for gene silencing in ATTR.
  3. On Vyndaqel competition: "Our gene silencing mechanism addresses the root cause; stabilizers slow but don't stop disease progression" — a direct mechanistic superiority argument that will define the competitive narrative.
  4. On Attruby (BridgeBio) launch: "Multiple options validate the market; our mechanism is differentiated" — acknowledging new entrants while reframing competition as market validation rather than threat.

Key analyst Q&A signals:

  1. Analysts pushed hard on Wainua (CARDIO-TTRansform) timing — Alnylam deflected: "We focus on what we can control; AMVUTTRA's data is best-in-class." The confidence signals pre-planned CARDIO-TTRansform scenario strategies.
  2. The Vyndaqel competition question was framed around mechanism (stabilizer vs silencer), not price or convenience — suggesting the analyst community is starting to accept the silencer superiority narrative.
  3. No analyst questions on ONPATTRO decline, indicating the market has fully priced in the AMVUTTRA transition.

2. HELIOS-B Long-Term Data at ESC 2025 (August)

  • New data from HELIOS-B Phase 3 presented at European Society of Cardiology Congress 2025
  • Demonstrated vutrisiran's long-term benefit in ATTR-CM: sustained reduction in mortality and cardiovascular events
  • Reinforced the 28% risk reduction (overall) and 33% (monotherapy) from the pivotal analysis
  • This data is critical for European market access discussions and international label expansion

3. EU Approval Secured (June 2025)

  • European Commission approved AMVUTTRA for ATTR-CM in June 2025
  • Expands the commercial opportunity to EU5 markets (France, Germany, Italy, Spain, UK)
  • International PET tracking now needs to incorporate CM-specific messaging analysis alongside existing PN tracking

4. Competitive Landscape

  • ATTRUBY (acoramidis/BridgeBio): Continued rapid uptake in US -- approaching $150M quarterly run rate
  • VYNDAQEL US discontinuation confirmed for end of 2025; Pfizer consolidating to VYNDAMAX
  • Wainua (eplontersen): CARDIO-TTRansform trial primary completion date pushed to April 2026
  • Pfizer reported declining US ATTR sales QoQ -- first signs of competitive erosion

5. Competitor Earnings Intelligence — Q3 2025

Revenue Snapshot — Q3 2025

Company Brand Q3 2025 Revenue YoY Growth Key Signal
Pfizer Vyndaqel/Vyndamax ~$1.1B +20% TTR stabilizer dominance in ATTR-CM; $4B+ annual franchise
BridgeBio Attruby (acoramidis) ~$50M N/A (launched Jan 2025) Fast ramp; "superior cardiac biomarker improvements vs tafamidis" positioning
Ionis/AstraZeneca Wainua (eplontersen) ~$20M N/A (early launch) SC monthly antisense for hATTR-PN; awaiting CARDIO-TTRansform for ATTR-CM
AMVUTTRA $492M +103% (TTR franchise) Context: AMVUTTRA surpassed consensus by >40% on ATTR-CM launch strength

Pfizer — Vyndaqel/Vyndamax (~$1.1B, +20%)

  1. Tafamidis remains the dominant ATTR-CM therapy: $1.1B quarterly revenue ($4B+ annual run rate) reflects the enormous installed base of ATTR-CM patients on stabilizer therapy. Pfizer management emphasized "tafamidis is the established standard of care for ATTR-CM" — a defensive posture as AMVUTTRA's CM approval (Jan 2025) brings the first gene silencer into the cardiomyopathy market.

  2. ATTR-CM monopoly largely unchallenged until HELIOS-B: Pfizer's Q3 commentary acknowledged the competitive shift but positioned tafamidis as the therapy with the deepest real-world evidence and longest track record. The HELIOS-B data (ESC 2025) showing AMVUTTRA's mortality benefit is the first direct challenge to this narrative.

  3. VYNDAQEL discontinuation confirmed: Pfizer consolidating to VYNDAMAX capsule, simplifying the franchise. This is a logistical move, not a competitive concession, but signals Pfizer is streamlining for a prolonged competitive battle.

Implication for Alnylam: Pfizer's $4B+ ATTR franchise is the market AMVUTTRA is entering. The opportunity is enormous — even a 20% share shift from stabilizers to silencers would represent $800M+ in annual revenue. The key battleground is whether cardiologists will view gene silencing as mechanistically superior to stabilization, or whether the convenience of oral therapy maintains tafamidis dominance.

BridgeBio — Attruby (~$50M, launched Jan 2025)

  1. Fastest launch ramp in ATTR history: ~$50M in revenue within months of launch, with BridgeBio positioning Attruby as having "superior cardiac biomarker improvements vs tafamidis" from ATTRACTion-CM data. BridgeBio is targeting tafamidis-dissatisfied patients and new diagnoses.

  2. Next-gen stabilizer positioning: Attruby is positioned not against AMVUTTRA but against tafamidis — claiming to be the better stabilizer with faster onset (1 month vs 6+ months for tafamidis). This means Attruby initially fragments the stabilizer market rather than the silencer market.

  3. International ambitions early: Already planning launches via Bayer (EU) and Alexion (Japan). BridgeBio is building a global ATTR franchise from day one.

Implication for Alnylam: Attruby is more dangerous than it appears. In the short term, it fragments the stabilizer market (good for AMVUTTRA — stabilizer-to-stabilizer switching doesn't affect silencer adoption). In the long term, if Attruby establishes itself as the "better stabilizer," it creates a higher bar for patients to switch from oral stabilizer to injectable silencer. AMVUTTRA must compete on mechanism superiority (silencer > stabilizer) rather than just efficacy vs tafamidis.

Ionis/AstraZeneca — Wainua (~$20M, early launch)

  1. Slow launch in hATTR polyneuropathy: ~$20M in early revenue for the SC monthly antisense oligonucleotide. Wainua is currently approved only for hATTR-PN, where it competes directly with AMVUTTRA.

  2. CARDIO-TTRansform is the key catalyst: AZ/Ionis awaiting results from CARDIO-TTRansform for the ATTR-CM indication. Primary completion extended to April 2026, creating uncertainty about Wainua's cardiomyopathy future.

  3. If positive: Wainua becomes a direct AMVUTTRA competitor across both PN and CM — the only other gene silencer in the market. If negative: AMVUTTRA's silencer position in ATTR-CM is cemented for years.

Implication for Alnylam: CARDIO-TTRansform is the single biggest competitive catalyst for the next 12 months. Alnylam should be preparing two scenarios: one where AMVUTTRA is the sole silencer in CM (defensive positioning vs stabilizers only) and one where Wainua enters CM (offensive positioning on HELIOS-B superiority data).

Synthesis: Q3 2025 Competitive Earnings Picture

The ATTR-CM market now has three established therapeutic approaches: stabilizers (tafamidis ~$4B+, acoramidis ~$200M annualized), gene silencers (AMVUTTRA ~$2B annualized, Wainua ~$80M annualized in PN only), and the competitive dynamics are increasingly about patient profile matching. Stabilizers offer oral convenience; silencers offer mechanistic superiority (halting TTR production vs merely stabilizing the protein). AMVUTTRA's Q3 2025 performance ($492M, smashing consensus) proves cardiologists are receptive to the silencer approach — but $4B+ in stabilizer revenue shows the majority of patients remain on oral therapy.

5. International PET Implications

  • With EU CM approval, international PET messaging will need to evolve from PN-centric to include ATTR-CM messages
  • The Q4 2024 international PET data (Spain, Germany, Japan) already showed CM efficacy topics being discussed, anticipating the approval
  • France and Italy Q2 2024 data showed Alnylam still leading among neurologists but trailing among cardiologists -- this gap becomes more consequential with CM label

ZoomRx Research Status

  • US PET, ATU, PCA all active with expanded CM tracking
  • ATTRUBY added to PET tracking (90 interactions/quarter)
  • International PET Wave 2 deliverables completed for all five countries
  • Q1 2025 integrated analysis delivered, mapping to Alnylam's four strategic imperatives

Signals for Vinoth

  • AMVUTTRA ATTR-CM revenue trajectory suggests Alnylam will increase research investment. Expect scope expansion discussions
  • ATTRUBY tracking is now established -- BridgeBio may emerge as a separate research client opportunity
  • The EU CM approval creates demand for international ATU/PCA equivalents, not just PET
